comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around Meta's liability in a child exploitation case, the adequacy of the fine, and broader issues of social media regulation, privacy, and corporate accountability. Participants express skepticism about the effectiveness of penalties and the company's practices.

comments.author: sarbanharble
comments.text: It takes 7 clicks to turn off ads that promote eating disorders. Thats enough proof.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a specific issue with platform controls, emphasizing the need for better regulation of harmful content.

comments.author: exabrial
comments.text: That fine is missing a few zeros on the right side
comments.analysis: The comment dismisses the fine as insignificant without providing detailed context or analysis.

comments.author: zeeshana07x
comments.text: Fines like this only work if they're large enough to change behavior. $375M for a company Meta's size is more of an accounting entry than a deterrent.
comments.analysis: This comment critically assesses the effectiveness of financial penalties, suggesting they are insufficient for large corporations.

comments.author: tombert
comments.text:
They had to pay about $375 million. That's a lot of money, but I suspect that Facebook has made considerably more than that on targeting children.

I'm hardly the first person to use this logic, but if they make more money breaking the law than they have to pay in fines, then it's not a fine, it's a business expense.
comments.analysis: The comment points out the potential profit motive behind the company's actions, implying fines are unlikely to be a strong deterrent.

comments.author: billfor
comments.text: and also https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=47514916 It might be good to roll all the comments together.
comments.analysis: This comment suggests aggregating related discussions without adding substantive analysis.

comments.author: cedws
comments.text: Wasn't Zuckerberg caught red handed in emails signing off on this? When is he going to be facing consequences?
comments.analysis: Raises a question about accountability but lacks evidence or detailed context to support the claim.

comments.author: deepsun
comments.text:
I cheer any decision that holds any private web property (like Facebook) accountable for it's user actions.

It helps to reduce hegemony of large social platforms and promotes privately owned websites. For example, I know everyone who has permissions to post on my website (or pre-moderate strangers comments), and is ready to take responsibility for their posts, what my website publishes.

Currently the legal stance seems strange to me -- large media platforms are allowed to store, distribute, rank and sell strangers data, while at the same time they claim they are not responsible for it.
comments.analysis: Expresses support for accountability measures, emphasizing the importance of responsibility for online platforms.

comments.author: girishso
comments.text: Why do we call this company "Meta"? It's the same old "Facebook".
comments.analysis: A rhetorical question that criticizes rebranding without substantive critique.
